summary: Deep-read 7 central papers in parallel, extract structured notes tied to the research hypothesis

result.title: Emergent Response Planning in LLMs (Dong, Zhou, Liu, Yang, Lu; ICML 2025 / arXiv:2502.06258v3)
result.core_contribution: The paper shows that LLMs, despite next-token training, encode global attributes of their entire upcoming response in their prompt-level hidden representations BEFORE generating any tokens ("emergent response planning"). Using simple MLP probes on prompt representations, they predict structure (response length, reasoning steps), content (story character choice, final multiple-choice answer), and behavior (answer correctness/confidence, factual consistency) attributes of the full response. They provide the first formal definition of response planning and characterize how it scales with model size, evolves across layers, and across the generation timeline.
result.methodology:
Setup: An L-layer decoder LLM pi(y|x). They extract layer-wise hidden representations H^l_x at the prompt position (the position that produces the first response token y1) and train probes h_theta(H^l_x) -> g(y), where g(y) is an "attribute rule" summarizing the full greedy-decoded response (e.g., token count). If probing is non-trivially accurate, the model "planned" that attribute pre-generation.

Six tasks across three attribute categories, each paired with 2 datasets:
- Structure: response length (UltraChat, AlpacaEval; regression on token count); reasoning steps (GSM8K, MATH; regression on number of "Step k:" markers, capped at 8).
- Content: character choices (TinyStories, ROCStories; 4-class classification of the animal introduced, excluding animals in first two words); multiple-choice answers (CommonsenseQA, SocialIQA; 5-class A-E, model must give analysis FIRST then answer).
- Behavior: answer confidence = correctness (MedMCQA, ARC-Challenge; binary correct/incorrect vs ground truth); factual consistency (CREAK, FEVER; binary, match between model's agree/disagree stance and statement truth).

Probes: one-hidden-layer MLPs, ReLU, hidden size grid {1,2,4,8,16,32,64,128,256,512,1024}; 400 epochs; MSELoss (regression), CrossEntropyLoss (classification); 60/20/20 train/val/test; grid search over hidden size AND layer; best-hyperparameter test scores; averaged over 3 seeds. Classes strictly balanced so accuracy = F1.

Spurious-correlation control ("first-token shortcut"): prompts engineered so the target attribute is NOT inferable from the immediate next token, e.g., multiple-choice and confidence tasks force the model to write analysis BEFORE the final answer, so initial tokens do not reveal the choice.

Data collection/labeling: greedy-decode responses; base-model outputs delimited by [END OF RESPONSE]; pattern-match to extract labels; data augmentation by truncating responses several tokens before the key info appears and recomputing labels, keeping original+augmented in same split. Representations collected via forward pass at truncation point (all layers).

Ablations: (5.1) scaling across Llama-2-chat 7B/13B/70B, Llama-3-Instruct 8B/70B, Qwen2-Instruct 7B/72B, Qwen2.5-Instruct 1.5B/32B/72B on UltraChat+TinyStories; (5.2) probing at equidistant positions along the generation timeline (TinyStories character choice, MedMCQA confidence); (5.3) verbalized self-estimate vs probe (response length via "[TOKENS]number[/TOKENS]" prompt; character choice).
result.datasets: UltraChat and AlpacaEval (response length); GSM8K and MATH (reasoning steps); TinyStories and ROCStories (character choices, 4-class top-4 animals); CommonsenseQA and SocialIQA (multiple-choice answers, 5-class); MedMCQA and ARC-Challenge (answer confidence/correctness, binary); CREAK and FEVER (factual consistency, binary). Response length capped at 1000 tokens, reasoning steps capped at 8. All datasets via HuggingFace (links in Appendix A.2). Class-balanced after augmentation. Exact sizes N not stated numerically.
result.baselines: Random baseline (dashed line) for classification F1 (0.5 for binary confidence/factual-consistency, 0.25 for 4-class character, 0.20 for 5-class MC). For verbalized comparison: model's own self-estimate via direct prompting (Section 5.3) used as a baseline against probing. Base models vs instruction-tuned models compared throughout as the central contrast. No external probing-method baseline; the conceptual baselines are Future Lens (Pal et al. 2023), Geva et al. 2023, Men et al. 2024, Wu et al. 2024 (cited, not re-run).
result.metrics: Regression: Spearman (S), Kendall (K), Pearson (P) correlation coefficients between predicted and true attribute. Classification: F1 score (= accuracy due to strict class balance). Layer-wise analysis uses absolute + row-normalized performance heatmaps. Verbalization gap measured as Spearman/F1 difference between Self-Estimate and Probing.
result.key_results:
In-dataset probing (Fig 2): response length Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.66/S=0.85/P=0.85; reasoning steps Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.67/S=0.82/P=0.79. Instruction-tuned > base on structure attributes. Base models weaker but positive on length (e.g., Qwen2-7B K=0.41/S=0.57; Llama-3-8B base K=0.29/S=0.41 — weakest). Content/behavior classification clearly above random for BOTH base and tuned models.

Cross-dataset (Fig 3, 10): correlations drop but stay strong (e.g., UltraChat->AlpacaEval length Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.46/S=0.64; base Qwen2-7B drops to K=0.20/S=0.30; Llama-2-7B base K=0.15/S=0.23). GSM8K->MATH reasoning steps Qwen2-7B-Instruct K=0.51/S=0.66.

Probe saliency (Fig 4): performance plateaus by hidden size <=128; behavior attributes plateau ~8, structure ~16, content ~32 — a complexity hierarchy.

Layer dynamics (Fig 5): structure attributes peak in MIDDLE layers then partially diminish; content attributes peak in LATER layers; behavior attributes are uniform/stable from early layers.

Scaling (Fig 6): within a family, bigger = better planning (length slope 0.042 Spearman, character slope 0.019 F1 across 1.5B-72B), but does NOT generalize across families.

Generation-timeline dynamics (Fig 7): three-phase U-shape — HIGH accuracy at start (global plan), DECLINE in middle (local token focus), RECOVERY near end. (MedMCQA confidence F1 ~0.70 at start -> ~0.55 mid -> ~0.70 end; TinyStories character ~0.80 -> ~0.65 -> ~0.85.)

Verbalization gap (Fig 8): probing >> self-estimate. Response length probing Spearman ~0.80-0.85 vs self-estimate 0.15-0.49 for tuned models; base models near or below random (Llama-2-7B -0.03, Llama-3-8B -0.04). Character choice probing F1 ~0.72-0.86 vs self-estimate 0.07-0.31. Models encode more than they can verbalize.
result.relevance_to_hypothesis:
Strongly relevant and partly supportive of the hypothesis that models pre-plan their answer/turn-ending. Direct evidence the model commits to global response properties (including final answer, character, and response LENGTH) from the prompt representation before generating, i.e. pre-planning of answer AND of turn length. This is the mechanistic substrate the hypothesis posits would reduce reactivity.

BUT: (1) The paper compares INSTRUCTION-TUNED vs BASE models, NOT RL/RLHF specifically vs base; it provides no RL-vs-base contrast on reactivity. It finds tuned > base on planning STRUCTURE attributes (length, steps), with content/behavior planning present even in base models (attributed to pre-training). This is suggestive that fine-tuning strengthens length/structure commitment — partially consistent with "RL-tuned models pre-plan more" — but RLHF is not isolated.
(2) The three-phase timeline result (Fig 7) is the key nuance for "reactivity": planning is strongest at the start (committed plan), weakens mid-generation, and re-strengthens at the end. The early-strong "global planning intent" supports the idea of a committed end-of-turn plan; the mid-decline could be read as a window of local flexibility.
(3) The paper does NOT test reaction to NEW information / feedback mid-stream, and does NOT test rotating certainty across question parts or distributing uncertainty — these are gaps the hypothesis could fill. The "answer confidence/correctness" probe shows the model encodes whether it will be right/wrong before answering, which is adjacent to "uncertainty across question parts."
(4) Greedy decoding only; they explicitly flag distributional/uncertainty-aware probing as open future work (Section 6.1), and propose "planning-aware training" and consistency losses (6.4) — directly adjacent to the hypothesis.
result.code_links: No GitHub repo for the paper's own code is given. Data/model links (HuggingFace) listed in Appendix A.1/A.2: models meta-llama/Llama-2-7b(-chat)-hf, Llama-2-13b/70b-chat-hf, Meta-Llama-3-8B(/-70B-Instruct), mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1 and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, Qwen/Qwen2-7B(-Instruct), Qwen2-72B-Instruct, Qwen/Qwen2.5-1.5B/32B/72B-Instruct. Datasets: hf datasets stingning/ultrachat, tatsu-lab/alpaca, openai/gsm8k, deepmind/math_dataset, roneneldan/TinyStories, Ximing/ROCStories, tau/commonsense_qa, allenai/social_i_qa, openlifescienceai/medmcqa, allenai/ai2_arc, amydeng2000/CREAK, fever/fever. (Note: a likely typo — Llama-3-8B-Instruct row links to Llama-2-7b-hf.)
result.reusable_for_experiment: Highly reusable. PROBING TECHNIQUE: train small MLP probes (hidden grid up to 128, 400 epochs, per-layer grid search, 3 seeds, 60/20/20 split) on prompt/representation activations to predict full-response attributes — directly transferable to test pre-commitment vs reactivity. METRICS: Spearman/Kendall/Pearson for continuous attributes; balanced F1/accuracy for categorical answers; the Self-Estimate-vs-Probing gap protocol. DATASETS for an answer-pre-planning / uncertainty experiment: CommonsenseQA & SocialIQA (5-way MC with forced analysis-before-answer — ideal for probing committed answer before reasoning finishes), MedMCQA & ARC-Challenge (binary correctness/confidence probing — can probe whether the model "knows the answer it wants to give"), CREAK & FEVER (stance/factual consistency). The forced "analysis then answer" prompt templates (Appendix A.3.1, Tasks 4-5) are reusable to block first-token shortcuts and to create a setting where new info arrives mid-turn. The generation-timeline probing protocol (probe at equidistant truncation points; Fig 7) is directly reusable to measure how/when commitment hardens and whether RL models harden earlier than base. The truncation-based data augmentation method is reusable. To test the rotating-certainty/uncertainty-distribution idea, one could adapt the answer-confidence probe across multi-part questions and use the proposed (unimplemented) distributional probing (predict label distributions, average over ~10 samples) from Section 6.1.

result.title: On Reasoning Strength Planning in Large Reasoning Models (NeurIPS 2025; arXiv:2506.08390v2)
result.core_contribution: The paper shows that large reasoning models (LRMs) "pre-plan" how much reasoning they will do BEFORE generating a single reasoning token: the number of reasoning tokens is linearly predictable from the question's activation at the start-of-reasoning <think> position (Spearman R > 0.8). It further shows this plan is encoded as a single "pre-allocated direction vector" in activation space whose magnitude (L2 norm) grows with question difficulty and causally controls reasoning length via activation steering. The vector controls length largely by modulating the logit of the end-of-reasoning </think> token.
result.methodology: Two probes of a "pre-planning" hypothesis. (1) Linear probing for reasoning length: extract the d-dim residual-stream activation h^(l) at the last token position = the start-of-reasoning <think> token (before any reasoning is generated), at each layer l. Fit Lasso linear regression (scikit-learn, regularization alpha=10) to predict y = number of tokens between <think> and </think>. 9:1 train/test split on MATH. Report Spearman R per layer. (2) Difference-in-means direction extraction: compute r_{i<-1}^(l) = mean activation of difficulty-level-i questions minus mean activation of difficulty-level-1 questions, for i=2..5, at the <think> position, per layer. Analyze pairwise cosine similarity of the four vectors and their L2 norms vs difficulty. (3) Causal activation steering: h'^(l) = h^(l) + lambda * r^(l), where r^(l) is the mean of the four difference vectors; sweep lambda from -0.2 to +0.2 in 0.05 steps; applied at each layer and each token position. Measure reasoning token count, answer token count, and accuracy. (4) Link probe to direction: feed r^(l) through the trained predictor (yhat = r W + b) to show the vector yields positive predicted length. (5) Mechanism: measure shift in logit of </think> under steering, compared against the EOS token <|endoftext|> and 500 randomly selected tokens; also measure logits of reflection tokens (Wait, Hmm, Oh, Alright). Control experiment: directly scale </think> logit by factor gamma (0.8, 1, 2) to show naive logit editing is unstable. Generation setup: vLLM, temperature 0.6, max new tokens 16,384, 8 rollouts averaged; prompt = "Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within \boxed{}." 8 A100 GPUs, ~80 A100 GPU-days. Applications: (a) overthink detection — predict length before generation on 100 AlpacaEval vanilla questions vs their overthink-attack variants; (b) efficient inference — apply negative steering on easy questions (MMLU 100 single-choice questions, MATH500 Level-1) to cut tokens while preserving accuracy.
result.datasets: MATH (Hendrycks et al.; 5 difficulty levels) — primary probing/direction dataset, 9:1 split. MATH500 (subset) — steering accuracy eval. AIME 2024 — steering eval. OlympiadBench — steering eval. MMLU (100 sampled single-choice questions, lm-evaluation-harness setting) — efficient-inference eval. AlpacaEval (100 sampled vanilla questions + overthink-attack variants) — overthink detection. GPQA diamond — generalization steering. LiveCodeBench (Code Execution and Code Generation) — generalization steering. Overthink attack from Kumar et al. (arXiv 2502.02542) used to construct overthinking variants.
result.baselines: No competing method baselines (mechanistic study). Internal comparisons/controls: (a) probe correlation across layers and model sizes; (b) impact on </think> logit vs EOS token and 500 random tokens; (c) steering at lambda=0 (no steering) as the reference for accuracy tables; (d) direct logit-scaling by gamma (0.8/1/2) as an alternative to steering, shown to be unstable. Reasoning-length steering directions from prior work (ThinkEdit, SEAL, representation engineering) cited as related but those derive directions from full CoT answers, not from pre-generation question activations.
result.metrics: Spearman correlation R between predicted and actual reasoning-token count (probe quality). Cosine similarity between difference-in-means direction vectors. L2 norm of direction vectors (vs difficulty). Reasoning token number, answer token number (post-</think>). Accuracy (%) on math/code/QA benchmarks. Logit value of </think> / EOS / random / reflection tokens under steering.
result.key_results: Probe: Spearman R > 0.8 across model sizes; reported 0.8421 on R1-Distill-Qwen (MATH, Figure 1a); R1-Distill-Llama-8B middle layers R ~ 0.842-0.848. Prediction accuracy increases with layer depth, plateauing in later layers. Direction vectors: pairwise cosine similarity ~0.99 (near 1.0 in final layers), indicating a single shared direction; L2 norm increases monotonically with difficulty, ~proportional to extra reasoning tokens needed. Steering accuracy gains (lambda positive, Table 1, avg over MATH500/AIME2024/OlympiadBench): R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B 51.84->52.42 (+0.58); 7B 66.91->68.82 (+1.91, AIME +4.58); 14B 72.37->74.72 (+2.35, AIME +5.83); 32B 73.75->75.43 (+1.68); QwQ-32B 64.19->64.65 (+0.46). Negative steering monotonically reduces reasoning tokens and accuracy while leaving answer-token count roughly unchanged. Mechanism: negative steering raises </think> logit (more likely to stop -> fewer tokens), positive steering lowers it; effect on </think> far exceeds EOS and random tokens. Reflection-token logits (Wait, Hmm, Oh, Alright) rise with positive steering. Naive logit scaling gamma=0.8 collapses (accuracy ~0, answer tokens=2, runaway reasoning ~16k tokens), showing logit shift is a result, not cause, of planning. Case study (R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, 1-2+3-...-100): same correct answer -50 but 2253 tokens unsteered vs 113 tokens steered. GPQA diamond and LiveCodeBench show token count tracks lambda and proper positive steering can improve accuracy (e.g., 32B GPQA 55.76->59.77).
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: STRONGLY supportive of the "pre-planning" premise of our hypothesis. The paper provides direct activation-level evidence that LRMs commit to a response-length budget (reasoning strength) BEFORE generating any reasoning token — exactly the "pre-plan the end of their next turn" mechanism in our hypothesis. The plan is concretely realized as a single linear direction whose magnitude scales with anticipated difficulty and causally sets where the model will emit </think>. Key nuances for our hypothesis: (1) All studied models are RL/distillation-trained LRMs (DeepSeek-R1 distills, QwQ-32B trained via RL); the paper does NOT compare against non-reasoning base LLMs, so it does not directly test the RL-vs-base differential our hypothesis needs — this is a gap we would fill. (2) The plan is set from the QUESTION activation alone, implying commitment to length before seeing how reasoning unfolds; this is consistent with reduced reactivity to information discovered mid-reasoning. (3) The L2-norm/difficulty coupling means the model maps a single scalar (difficulty/uncertainty) onto a global length budget rather than distributing uncertainty across question parts — relevant to our idea of rotating certainty across sub-questions. (4) The paper frames "overthinking" on easy questions as MIS-allocated pre-planned strength, i.e., the pre-plan can be wrong and is not corrected during generation — indirect evidence of failure to revise. It does NOT study multi-turn reactivity or answer pre-planning under new information, leaving our specific reactivity claim untested.
result.code_links: Code: https://github.com/AlphaLab-USTC/LRM-plans-CoT . Other URLs referenced: model https://huggingface.co/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B ; eval https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness ; diff-in-means method https://blog.eleuther.ai/diff-in-means/ . Related "Emergent response planning in LLM" (Dong et al., arXiv:2502.06258) is the closest cited work on pre-planning answer attributes (confidence, MCQ choice).
result.reusable_for_experiment: Directly reusable to TEST our hypothesis: (1) Linear-probe-on-<think>-activation technique (Lasso, alpha=10, last-token position, predict y=token count) — adapt to probe whether the ANSWER (not just length) is pre-encoded, and compare RL-tuned LRM vs its base model. (2) Difference-in-means + activation-steering pipeline (h' = h + lambda*r, lambda in [-0.2,0.2]) to causally test commitment/reactivity. (3) </think>-logit-tracking and reflection-token-logit method to detect where/whether the model decides to stop. (4) Datasets/benchmarks: MATH (5 difficulty levels — ideal for "rotating certainty"), MATH500, AIME2024, OlympiadBench, GPQA diamond, MMLU, LiveCodeBench, AlpacaEval; overthink-attack construction (Kumar et al. 2502.02542) for stress-testing. (5) Models: full DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen series (1.5B/7B/14B/32B), QwQ-32B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B — RL/distilled LRMs whose non-reasoning Qwen2.5 / Llama-3 bases are available for the RL-vs-base contrast our hypothesis requires. (6) Generation config (vLLM, temp 0.6, max 16384, 8 rollouts) and \boxed{} prompt for reproducibility. (7) Closest method to extend for "answer pre-planning / reactivity" is the cited Dong et al. 2502.06258 emergent-response-planning probes (pre-planning answer confidence and MCQ choice).

result.title: Do Language Models Plan Ahead for Future Tokens? (Wu, Morris, Levine; COLM 2024)
result.core_contribution: The paper asks whether transformers "think ahead" by preparing information at position t for use at future positions t+τ, and distinguishes two mechanisms: "pre-caching" (the model deliberately computes features useful for the future at a cost to the present) vs. "breadcrumbs" (features useful for the present incidentally also help the future, with no deliberate trade-off). It introduces "myopic training" — gradient descent with the off-diagonal (future-loss-to-past-weights) gradient terms removed — and defines a "myopia gap" (loss difference between myopic and vanilla models) as a quantitative measure of pre-caching. Finding: a synthetic task forces clear pre-caching, but natural-language models (GPT-2, small Pythia) mostly use breadcrumbs; pre-caching grows with model scale (non-negligible by Pythia 2.8B).
result.methodology: Theory: For a causal seq-to-seq model with weights tied across positions, total loss gradient is a sum over an upper-triangular Jacobian; off-diagonal terms i<j (gradient of future loss j w.r.t. past weights i) are the training signal that produces pre-caching. "Myopic descent" removes these off-diagonal terms (implemented via a "myopic attention mechanism" that feeds past key/value states from a cloned-and-detached copy of previous hidden states, so gradients do not flow to past positions). Definitions: untied/tied myopia gap (loss gap between best myopic and best vanilla model), and local myopia bonus (per-model violation of myopia constraints). Theorem 13 proves myopic descent converges to a myopic solution under forward-bias + strong convexity + smoothness. Three model variants implemented with the myopic attention mechanism: (1) myopic-descent model (detached past states), (2) "local myopic" model (past key/value states supplied by a separate frozen pretrained vanilla model), (3) "transformer bigram" baseline (past key/value states zeroed out). Synthetic experiment (Dp task): regression dataset where y_n = z_n * sum_{i=1}^{a} sin(b*x_{n-i}) + (1-z_n)*x_n, with x~N(0,1), z~Bernoulli(p), a=b=10, N=64. Designed so the model benefits from pre-caching sin(b*x_n) for future use. Two-layer transformer (embd dim 128, 2 heads, n_inner 512, ReLU, AdamW lr 1e-3, Huber loss), trained vanilla vs myopic on 30M length-64 sequences per p in {0.01,0.1,0.3,1}. Linear probes (R^2) and per-neuron correlations fit on hidden states (50,000 eval samples) to detect whether sin(b*x_{n-i}) is computed/stored. Natural language: GPT-2-small (124M; 12 layers, 12 heads, 768 dim, 50257 vocab, CrossEntropy, AdamW lr 6e-4) trained from scratch one epoch on 4.6M MS MARCO sequences truncated to length 64; evaluated per-position on a sliding window over PG-19. Scaling: Pythia suite 14M-2.8B finetuned (from pretrained checkpoints) one epoch on 10M length-64 sequences from The Pile, vanilla vs myopic, evaluated on LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy and validation cross-entropy. Multiplication experiment: GPT-2-small trained on natural-number multiplication (space-delimited digits, reverse digit order, zero-padded), 0/1 exact-match accuracy on 8x8 digit-count grid; filler-token study compares zero-padding to length 5 vs 10. Gradient-angle study: on Pythia-410M training checkpoints, measures norms of myopic vs future gradient components and their cosine similarity over training.
result.datasets: Synthetic Dp regression task (Dp = D^64_{p,10,10}, 30M training sequences, 50K eval samples). MS MARCO (Nguyen et al. 2016), 4.6M sequences truncated to length 64 (GPT-2 training). PG-19 (Rae et al. 2019), 100K-token sample / sliding window for per-position loss eval. The Pile (Gao et al. 2020), 10M length-64 sequences subsampled for Pythia finetuning. Natural-number multiplication synthetic dataset (10M examples, up to 8 digits; 1024 validation examples per digit-count pair). NLU benchmarks: LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy.
result.baselines: Vanilla (ordinary) gradient descent model vs. myopic-descent model (the central comparison). "Local myopic" model (past hidden states from a separate frozen vanilla model — focuses fully on next-token prediction). "Transformer bigram" model (off-diagonal key/value states zeroed; no use of past at all). Trivial constant-zero predictor for the synthetic regression (Huber loss 1.26). On the Dp task, the hand-designed Algorithm 1 (pre-caching) vs Algorithm 2 (brute force, no pre-caching) are conceptual baselines.
result.metrics: Myopia gap (cross-entropy difference between myopic and vanilla); local myopia bonus; (normalized) Huber loss L/p for regression; linear-probe R^2; per-neuron Pearson correlations; per-position cross-entropy loss; downstream accuracy (LAMBADA/PIQA/SciQ/ARC-Easy); 0/1 exact-match accuracy on multiplication; gradient component norms (myopic vs future) and cosine similarity between them.
result.key_results: Synthetic Dp: large myopia gap — normalized Huber loss L/p for vanilla vs myopic was 0.096 vs 1.10 (p=0.01), 0.016 vs 0.97 (p=0.1), 0.0030 vs 1.03 (p=0.3), 0.0074 vs 1.26 (p=1.0); trivial baseline = 1.26. Probes confirm layer-1 hidden state encodes sin(b*x_n) and layer-2 encodes the running sum, i.e. clear pre-caching. GPT-2 (MS MARCO): vanilla 3.28, myopic 3.40, local myopic 3.26, transformer bigram 5.33 cross-entropy. So myopia gap = 0.12, local myopia bonus = 0.02 — both small, and far smaller than the 5.33-3.28=2.05 bigram gap, supporting breadcrumbs. Per-position (Fig 4): myopic GPT-2 is BETTER than vanilla at sequence start (it can devote all compute to the next token) but falls behind as context grows; myopia gap rises monotonically with position to ~0.12. Scaling (Pythia): cross-entropy myopia gap grows with size, becoming clearly nonzero from 70M up and non-negligible by 2.8B; vanilla beats myopic on all four benchmarks with the gap widening at larger scale. Multiplication: vanilla > myopic, especially for larger digit counts (e.g., large vanilla-minus-myopic accuracy gaps in the mid/high digit region). Filler tokens: padding multiplicands from length 5 to 10 IMPROVES vanilla accuracy but HURTS myopic accuracy — myopic models cannot exploit filler-token computation. Gradient angles (Pythia-410M): myopic gradient norm consistently exceeds future gradient norm throughout training (training dominated by immediate next-token signal); cosine similarity between myopic and future gradients varies (roughly -0.5 to +0.2) over training.
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: Strongly relevant as a conceptual/methodological foundation but does NOT directly study RL-fine-tuned models or answer-commitment. Their "pre-caching" is exactly the mechanism by which a model could "pre-plan" future tokens/answer; the myopia gap quantifies how much a model commits compute now to future outputs. Key takeaways for the hypothesis: (a) Pre-planning ("pre-caching") demonstrably exists and INCREASES with model scale — larger models plan further ahead, consistent with the intuition that more capable (and RL-tuned) models commit to answers earlier. (b) The "future-token meta-prediction" literature they build on (Pal et al. Future Lens, Hernandez et al., logit/tuned lens) shows future outputs are linearly decodable from a single hidden state — a directly usable probing approach to test whether an RL model has already "decided" its answer before generating it. (c) The myopia gap by position result shows models commit increasing future-relevant computation as the sequence lengthens, which could be reframed as reduced reactivity to late-arriving information. Caveats: the paper uses only base / pretrained / lightly-finetuned models (GPT-2, Pythia) — no RLHF/RL models — and does not test reaction to new information, answer revision, or uncertainty across question parts. So it informs (a) and the mechanism of pre-planning but provides no direct evidence on RL-vs-base differences, reactivity, or distributing uncertainty across question parts.
result.reusable_for_experiment: Reusable methods: (1) The "myopic attention mechanism" / myopic-training scheme and the myopia gap and local myopia bonus metrics — could be applied to RL-fine-tuned vs base models to measure whether RL increases pre-caching/pre-planning. (2) The "local myopic" model construction (swap in frozen past states) as a counterfactual probe of how much a forward pass relies on pre-cached info. (3) Linear probing of hidden states to predict FUTURE tokens (R^2 / correlation methodology; cf. Future Lens by Pal et al. and Hernandez et al. linear relation decoding) — directly usable to test if an RL model has pre-committed to an answer token before emitting it. (4) Per-position / per-token loss analysis to measure how commitment-to-future grows with position (proxy for declining reactivity to new context). (5) The filler-token / zero-padding paradigm to test whether models perform hidden lookahead computation. Reusable datasets/benchmarks: LAMBADA, PIQA, SciQ, ARC-Easy (multiple-choice QA, suitable for answer-commitment probing); MS MARCO, The Pile, PG-19 for LM-loss analysis; the multiplication exact-match task and synthetic Dp generator for controlled pre-caching experiments. Model suites: Pythia 14M-2.8B with public training checkpoints (good for scale studies and gradient-angle analysis); GPT-2-small. For the specific hypothesis we would need to ADD RLHF/RL-tuned model variants and design multi-part / new-information-injection question sets, which the paper does not provide.

result.title: Are You Sure? Challenging LLMs Leads to Performance Drops in The FlipFlop Experiment (Laban, Murakhovs'ka, Xiong, Wu; Salesforce AI Research, 2024)
result.core_contribution: The paper introduces the FlipFlop experiment, a two-to-three-turn simulated conversation that quantifies LLM sycophancy on classification tasks: round 1 the model answers, round 2 a "challenger" utterance (e.g. "Are you sure?") invites reconsideration, and the model either confirms or flips. Across 10 LLMs, 7 tasks, and 5 challengers (67,640 conversations), models flip answers 46% of the time and lose 17% accuracy on average between initial and final prediction (the "FlipFlop effect"). Finetuning Mistral-7b on balanced synthetic challenge data reduces but does not eliminate the effect (60% reduction).
result.methodology: 7-step procedure (Fig 2): (1) user sends zero-shot classification prompt; (2) LLM gives initial answer via greedy decoding, max 200 tokens (median 10 words / mean 29); (3) user sends challenger utterance; (4) LLM responds (median 36 / mean 67 words); (5) rule-based label extraction from both turns — if model answers affirmatively, initial=final; (6) if extraction fails (rambling), a 3rd confirmation turn "OK. What is your final answer to the initial question?" is added; (7) confirmation response (median 27 / mean 46 words). Label extraction succeeds in 95+% of initial responses, only 82% of challenger responses, raised to 97% with the confirmation turn. Uses a "generate+extract" method (free-form generation + rule-based label extraction) rather than the "logprob" method used in MMLU, arguing it better simulates conversational behavior. Temperature=0 (greedy) for the core experiment; an appendix temperature sweep (GPT-3.5-Turbo on SciQ, T=0.0 to 2.0 in 0.2 steps) shows both initial accuracy degrades and FlipFlop effect magnitude grows ~0.3 per 0.1 temperature increase, so T=0 is a conservative (lower-bound) setting. Challengers must be "confirmatory" (an affirmative "Yes" should confirm) and are designed to encourage reflection, not coerce. Metrics: Acc_init, Acc_final, FlipFlop effect (Delta_FF = Acc_final - Acc_init), FLIP indicator, flip probabilities Any->Flip, Correct->Flip, Wrong->Flip, and %Sorry (apologetic-keyword presence). (Model,task) pairs where initial accuracy is <5% above random are excluded (performance-based selection). Finetuning (Sec 6): generate 10,000 synthetic non-sycophantic FlipFlop conversations balanced so correct-flipping occurs in 50% of cases; token masking so model learns only from the last assistant response; QLoRA, 1 epoch, batch size 8, single A100. Five variants A-E vary #tasks (1 vs 3), #challengers (1 vs 40), inclusion of Dolly instruction data, and model-specific error filtering.
result.datasets: Seven evaluation classification tasks (sample counts): Logical Fallacy from BIG-bench (100 samples, Valid/Invalid, 50 each); TruthfulQA (Lin et al. 2022, 400 samples, multiple-choice, 38 categories); New Yorker Captions (Hessel et al. 2023, 100 samples, 4-choice humor); ARC-Challenge (Clark et al. 2018, 400 samples, Challenge subset); SummEdits (Laban et al. 2023, 100 samples, consistent/inconsistent summary factuality, 5+5 across 10 domains); SciQ (Welbl 2017, 100 samples, multiple-choice science, no context paragraph); LegalBench-CCQA / Consumer Contracts QA (Guha et al. 2023, 100 samples, Yes/No). Finetuning synthetic data built from CSQA (Saha et al. 2018), BoolQ (Clark et al. 2019), Logical Fallacy, plus Dolly (Conover et al. 2023) instruction data. Models: 3 open-source — Llama2-7b (meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf), Llama2-13b (Llama-2-13b-chat-hf), Mistral-7b (mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1); 7 proprietary — Cohere Command-XL (command-xlarge), Claude V1.3 (claude-v1.3), Claude V2 (claude-2), PaLM2-Bison (text-bison@001), Gemini-Pro, GPT-3.5-Turbo (gpt-3.5-turbo), GPT-4 (gpt-4). API runs Oct 1 - Nov 10, 2023.
result.baselines: Random-prediction baseline per task (mean 39.3% across tasks). The base (non-finetuned) Mistral-7b-Instruct is the baseline for finetuning experiments. The 10 models are compared against each other; PaLM-Bison and GPT-4 act as low-sycophancy reference points. Comparison drawn to prior sycophancy work (Perez et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2023; Sharma et al. 2023) and self-correction work (Huang et al. 2023a).
result.metrics: Initial accuracy (Acc_init), final accuracy (Acc_final), FlipFlop effect Delta_FF = Acc_final - Acc_init; flip rates Any->Flip = P(FLIP), Correct->Flip = P(FLIP | initially correct), Wrong->Flip = P(FLIP | initially wrong); %Sorry (apology keyword rate); distribution buckets (major drop <=-10, minor drop (-10,-2], no change (-2,2], minor gain (2,10], major gain >=10); directional flip rate (positive->negative label).
result.key_results: Across all models: average flip rate 46%, average Delta_FF = -17%. Per-model Delta_FF (Table 2): Claude V1.3 -35.1, Claude V2 -24.8, Gemini-Pro -21.6, GPT-3.5-Turbo -19.7, Llama2-13b -16.8, Mistral-7b -14.5, Llama2-7b -13.7, GPT-4 -6.4, PaLM-bison -5.5, Command-XL -1.3. Flip rates highest for Llama2-7b (69.4%), lowest PaLM-bison (10.3%), GPT-4 (12.8%), Command-XL (14.7%). For ALL models Correct->Flip < Wrong->Flip (interpreted as more uncertainty when initially wrong). Delta_FF strongly correlated with flip rate (rho ~ -0.78) but only moderately with Acc_init (rho = 0.52). Challenger ranking by harm: IDTS -22.9, TEACH -22.8, PHD -17.9, AUS -8.1, ABS -7.2 (most-harmful ~3x least-harmful; persona challengers among top 3). Task ranking: LegalBench-CCQA -30.1 (worst, ~8x), SciQ -19.4, SummEdits -15.7, ARC-C -15.7, NY Captions -12.2, TruthfulQA -9.9, Logical Fallacy -4.9. 73% of experiments show minor/major deterioration; only PaLM-bison and GPT-4 show no change in a majority. Directional flips (Table 3): models show imbalanced flipping — on SummEdits 8/10 models preferentially flip consistent->inconsistent (cautious labeling). Finetuning (Table 4): base Mistral Delta_FF -9.5 (all tasks) / -18.5 (hard subset); best overall Mistral-7b-D (3 tasks, 40 challengers, +Dolly) -3.8 (60% reduction), hard subset -12 to -13; single-task variants A/B made it worse (-17 to -19). %Sorry dropped from base 60% to <2% for all finetuned variants. Error filtering (E) gave no significant benefit, contradicting Wei et al. 2023. Acc_init preserved (~60-64%) after finetuning.
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: Partial/indirect support. The paper directly measures LLM reactivity to new information (the challenger), which is the core of the hypothesis's "ability to react." However, the framing is inverted: here the failure mode is OVER-reacting (flipping correct answers) rather than under-reacting / pre-committing. It does NOT investigate internal pre-planning of answer or response-length before generation, nor probe hidden states — there is no mechanistic evidence about committing to an answer. On RL vs base: the paper notes RLHF is hypothesized as a sycophancy source (Perez 2022) but FINDS sycophancy is universal across models trained with and without RLHF, and that some models share little instruction/multi-turn data, explicitly questioning whether it also originates in pre-training — this WEAKENS a simple "RL-finetuning causes the deficit" claim. Notably the most sycophantic models are Claude V1.3/V2 and GPT-3.5 while GPT-4/PaLM-Bison resist, so heavy RLHF does not monotonically predict the effect. On uncertainty across answer parts: the Correct->Flip < Wrong->Flip result and directional/imbalanced flipping (Table 3) show models hold differential, label-dependent uncertainty — tangentially relevant to "rotating what is certain across a question." The paper does NOT test rotating certainty across a series of questions. Overall it provides a strong reactivity testbed and baseline behavior but no direct evidence on pre-planning/commitment.
result.code_links: Authors state "We plan to release code, and data upon acceptance" (footnote 2) — no concrete repo URL in the paper. Only third-party links present: HuggingFace Hub model IDs, github.com/anthropics/anthropic-sdk-python, docs.cohere.com/docs/the-cohere-platform, github.com/openai/openai-python, newyorker.com/cartoons/contest. arXiv:2311.08596v2.
result.reusable_for_experiment: Highly reusable: (1) The FlipFlop two/three-turn protocol itself as a reactivity probe — directly adaptable to test whether RL-finetuned vs base models react to mid-turn new information; (2) the 7 benchmark tasks (TruthfulQA, ARC-Challenge, SciQ, SummEdits, LegalBench-CCQA, BIG-bench Logical Fallacy, New Yorker Captions) with stated sample sizes (100-400) and short names; (3) the 5 core challengers (AUS, IDTS, ABS, TEACH, PHD) plus the full 40-challenger list in Appendix A.6 for synthetic data; (4) metrics: Delta_FF, Any/Correct/Wrong->Flip, %Sorry, directional positive->negative flip rate, the 5-bucket distribution — all reusable to quantify reactivity and to compare base vs RLHF models; (5) the generate+extract vs logprob distinction and the temperature-sweep design (T=0..2, step 0.2) to study sensitivity; (6) the balanced synthetic-conversation finetuning recipe (10k convs, 50% correct-flip, token-masked last response, QLoRA) as a mitigation baseline. To test the specific pre-planning hypothesis one would need to ADD hidden-state/answer-token probing and a "rotating certainty across question parts" manipulation, which this paper does not provide. The Correct->Flip vs Wrong->Flip metric is a ready proxy for whether a model's commitment depends on its internal certainty.

result.title: A Simple "Try Again" Can Elicit Multi-turn LLM Reasoning (Unary Feedback as Observation, UFO)
result.core_contribution: The paper identifies that single-turn RLVR training (PPO, GRPO, DAPO, Dr. GRPO) collapses a model's multi-turn revision ability: RL-tuned models tend to repeat the exact same answer across turns instead of reacting to feedback. It introduces UFO (Unary Feedback as Observation), a method that converts static single-turn datasets into multi-turn RL episodes by injecting minimal negative feedback ("Try Again") after wrong answers, plus a turn-wise reward decay and answer-repetition penalty. UFO improves multi-turn success by up to 14% while preserving single-turn performance.
result.methodology: Multi-turn problem solving is framed as a finite-horizon MDP (S, A, P, R, T_max). State s_t = Concat(question q, history of {(answer_k, feedback_k)}). Feedback is restricted to NEGATIVE-only unary signals (e.g., "Try Again"); a correct answer terminates the episode, so no positive confirmation is ever added to history. Reward r_t = 1 if correct else 0. Training uses PPO (learned critic, GAE with gamma=lambda=1.0, Adam beta=(0.9,0.999), entropy coef 1e-3). Reward design for "adaptive reasoning": (1) reward decay R_t = gamma^t (gamma=0.5) to encourage MINIMALITY (fewer turns); (2) repetition penalty Penalty(tau)=lambda*(1 - E(tau)/T) based on number of EFFECTIVE (unique, never-before-seen) answers E(tau) over T turns, to encourage DIVERSITY; final R = R_t - Penalty(tau); small format penalty eta<0 for malformed output. Three reward schedules compared: exponential decay (gamma^n, gamma=0.5), linear decay (max(0,1-0.2(n-1))), constant (=1). Theory: Section 2.3 + Appendix B/C argue single-turn RL produces a peaked, low-entropy distribution; collision probability Coll(q)=sum q(y|x)^2 >= exp(-H(q)) (Jensen), so lower entropy forces higher repetition for a STATIC policy. Defines Parallel Policies (Pi_par, single-turn, sample i.i.d. ignoring feedback) vs Sequential Policies (Pi_seq, condition on full history); Pi_par subset of Pi_seq so max Succ@k(seq) >= max Succ@k(par) (Blackwell dominance); sequential policy can maintain rejection set H_t and approximate sampling-without-replacement, giving conditional success p'_t = p/(1 - sum p_i) > p. Training: Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct main model, PPO 200 optimization steps on A100, batch P=8 prompts x N=16 rollouts, T_max in {1,5,10}, validation T_max=5, episodes capped at 10 total actions. Prompt uses explicit <think>/<answer> tags, action budget "You have Y actions left", max length Z. A separate "tutor" model can also provide richer hints (Box 2). Case studies (Boxes 4-7) qualitatively trace pre-RL (adaptive but incomplete), post-single-turn-RL (identical repeated reasoning/answer), UFO success (incremental self-correction), and UFO "reasoning drift" (right answer via invalid steps).
result.datasets: Primary training: MMQ-Math = the MATH subset of MetaMathQA (Yu et al. 2024), augmented from MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021). Also trained on HotpotQA. Evaluation across 9 datasets in 4 domains: Math (MMQ-Math, TheoremQA [Chen 2023], GSM8K [Cobbe 2021]); STEM (GPQA [Rein 2023], MMLU-STEM [Hendrycks 2020, 15 STEM subjects]); QA (HotpotQA [multi-hop Wikipedia], ConcurrentQA [Arora 2022, temporal/causal]); General (MMLU [57 subjects], MMLU-Pro [Wang 2024b]). For repetition analysis, off-the-shelf RL models from HuggingFace (Table 2): DAPO-Qwen-32B, Qwen2.5-Math-7B/1.5B-Oat-Zero (Dr. GRPO), Qwen-2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B}-SimpleRL-Zoo (GRPO), Qwen2.5-7B-PPO-Zero, and their base Qwen models. Released model: LichengLiu03/Qwen2.5-3B-UFO.
result.baselines: Single-turn PPO-trained model with PARALLEL sampling (k independent completions, evaluated by Pass@k) is the main baseline vs UFO's sequential multi-turn (Succ@k). Also: base models without RL ("Base Model w/o RL"), single-turn "RL on MMQ-Math", and "RL on HotQA". Off-the-shelf RL algorithm comparisons for repetition: PPO, GRPO, DAPO, Dr. GRPO at scales 0.5B-32B. Ablations: T_max in {1,5,10}; reward schedules (constant/linear/exponential); feedback present in train+val vs train-only vs none; verbal feedback wording variants.
result.metrics: Effective answer / unique answer ratio = a response never explored in any prior turn (key diversity/reactivity metric). Pass@k (single-turn baseline). Succ@k = fraction of problems solved within k interaction turns (Succ@1 single-turn, Succ@5 and Succ@10 multi-turn). AvgTurns = mean number of turns to solve. Collision probability Coll(q) and Shannon entropy H(q) (theory). Number of actions/turns per episode (efficiency).
result.key_results: REPETITION after single-turn RL: in ~70% of failed cases the single-turn RL model gives EXACTLY the same answer across 5 turns (only 1 effective answer). Unique-answer ratio drops after RL across all methods/scales: DAPO 32B 78.0%->65.7%; Dr.GRPO 1.5B 38.0%->15.4% (largest drop); GRPO 0.5B 34.0%->24.3%; PPO 7B 57.6%->51.7%. UFO's own model: PPO+UFO 3B is the only one whose unique-answer ratio INCREASES after training (~84%->~94%). MAIN GAIN: 5-turn UFO improves multi-turn success up to 14% over single-turn RL with comparable inference cost. Table 1 (5-turn success) examples: Qwen2.5-3B MMQ-Math 79.7->88.5; MMLU 66.8->85.2; MMLU-Pro 48.3->60.9; Qwen2.5-7B MMQ-Math 85.1->93.0; TheoremQA 33.6->42.1; Llama3.2-3B MMQ-Math 86.7->92.2. Gains generalize out-of-domain (math, STEM, QA, general); math-trained generalizes better than QA-trained. T_max=5 and T_max=10 both give >6% relative improvement over single-turn; T_max=5 best, 10 no significant extra gain. Multi-turn training helps even at inference k=1 (better single-shot generalization). Providing explicit feedback gives >8% peak improvement; feedback only-in-training still helps (intrinsic gain). Exponential reward decay reduces mean number of actions ~10% with no loss in success. Effective-answer proportion rises from 80% to >90% over training. Robust across feedback wordings ("Incorrect.", "Please try again.", "Please think again.", "Let's try again.").
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: STRONGLY SUPPORTS the hypothesis that RL-fine-tuned LLMs lose reactivity to new information relative to base models. Direct evidence: (1) base/pre-RL models REVISE their answer across turns given feedback, whereas after single-turn RL they REPEAT the identical answer (Figure 1, Case 1 vs Case 2; ~70% identical-answer rate). (2) Unique-answer ratio consistently DROPS after RL across PPO/GRPO/DAPO/Dr.GRPO and scales 0.5B-32B (Figure 3) - the more an RL method sharpens the policy, the worse the reactivity (Dr.GRPO worst). (3) Mechanistic account: RL creates a peaked, low-entropy distribution; collision probability Coll(q) >= exp(-H(q)) means a near-deterministic policy is mathematically forced to repeat - this is the "committing to an answer" phenomenon. The qualitative cases show single-turn RL models commit to one interpretation/derivation in Turn 1 and replay it verbatim Turns 2-5, never reacting to "try again" - exactly the "pre-plan / no revision" failure. Regarding pre-planning the END/LENGTH of a turn: the paper does not probe internal answer-commitment or response-length planning directly (no hidden-state/logit probing of pre-commitment), so it does not test that specific mechanism, but its repetition-collapse evidence is the behavioral signature. On UNCERTAINTY across question parts: not directly addressed; the paper's lever is rotating FEEDBACK across turns rather than rotating what is certain across question parts, but UFO's "rejection set" / sampling-without-replacement framing is conceptually adjacent to forcing the model to distribute exploration. The "reasoning drift" failure (Case 4: correct final answer via invalid intermediate steps) is relevant evidence that answer-level reward can decouple the committed answer from faithful reasoning.
result.code_links: https://github.com/lichengliu03/unary-feedback ; project page unary-feedback.github.io ; released model LichengLiu03/Qwen2.5-3B-UFO (HuggingFace). Off-the-shelf models in Table 2 incl. Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B, BytedTsinghua-SIA/DAPO-Qwen-32B, sail/Qwen2.5-Math-{7B,1.5B}-Oat-Zero, hkust-nlp/Qwen-2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B}-SimpleRL-Zoo, RLHFlow/Qwen2.5-7B-PPO-Zero.
result.reusable_for_experiment: REUSABLE METRICS: "effective answer / unique answer ratio" (count of never-before-seen answers across N turns) is a clean, directly reusable measure of reactivity/answer-commitment to compare base vs RL models - precisely operationalizes "less ability to react to new information". Also Succ@k vs Pass@k gap, AvgTurns, and collision probability / output entropy H(q) as a proxy for how peaked/pre-committed the policy is. REUSABLE PROTOCOL: their multi-turn "Try Again" harness (UFO) turns ANY static single-turn QA/math dataset into a multi-turn reactivity probe - feed a wrong-answer-feedback loop and measure whether the model revises; this is a ready-made testbed for our hypothesis. REUSABLE DATASETS/BENCHMARKS: MMQ-Math (MATH subset of MetaMathQA), GSM8K, MATH, TheoremQA, GPQA, MMLU/MMLU-STEM/MMLU-Pro, HotpotQA, ConcurrentQA. REUSABLE MODEL PAIRS for base-vs-RL comparison at matched scale: Qwen2.5-{0.5B,1.5B,7B,14B} vs their SimpleRL-Zoo (GRPO) RL versions; Qwen2.5-Math vs Oat-Zero (Dr.GRPO); Qwen2.5-32B vs DAPO-Qwen-32B; plus their released base Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct vs Qwen2.5-3B-UFO. To TEST the specific "pre-planning answer/length" mechanism the paper leaves open, one could add hidden-state/early-token probing for answer commitment on top of their repetition setup. The "rotate what is certain across question parts" idea could be implemented as a variant of their multi-turn rollout where each turn fixes a different sub-component, using the same effective-answer metric to measure forced uncertainty distribution.

result.title: Training Language Models to Self-Correct via Reinforcement Learning (SCoRe)
result.core_contribution: SCoRe is a multi-turn online RL method that teaches a single LLM to intrinsically self-correct its own answers using entirely self-generated data, with no separate corrector model, teacher, or external/oracle feedback at test time. It is presented as the first method to achieve significantly positive intrinsic self-correction (positive delta between attempt 1 and attempt 2), achieving +15.6% self-correction gain on MATH and +9.1% on HumanEval over base Gemini models. Key mechanism: a two-stage RL design (Stage I decouples the two attempts by constraining the first attempt to the base model while optimizing the second; Stage II jointly optimizes both attempts with a "progress" reward bonus) to avoid "behavior collapse."
result.methodology:
Setup: intrinsic self-correction as a multi-turn MDP. Given problem x, the policy pi_theta produces a first attempt y1, then receives a fixed self-correction instruction p1 (which does NOT reveal correctness) and produces a second attempt y2. l=2 turns (one round of correction). Oracle reward (answer checker for MATH; unit tests for code) used in TRAINING only, never at test time. Binary rewards.

Base RL algorithm: REINFORCE policy gradient with KL penalty to a reference model (Ahmadian et al. 2024), i.e., the standard single-turn RLHF objective extended to multi-turn (hierarchical framework of Zhou et al. 2024 / ArCHer).

Two diagnosed failure modes of naive approaches (Section 4): (1) distribution shift - offline SFT models correct base-model mistakes seen in training data but fail on their own self-generated mistakes (Figure 5 shows self-generated validation correction rate DEGRADES while fixed-set rate improves); (2) behavior collapse - models learn to make minimal/no edits in turn 2 (measured via edit-distance-ratio histograms, Figure 4: STaR/SFT pile up near 0 edit distance). Standard multi-turn RL ALSO collapses: each turn's accuracy improves but Delta(t1,t2) stays flat and "different answer frequency" in turn 2 drops (Figure 6). Explanation framed as a meta-learning memorization problem (Yin et al. 2019): two equally-optimal training solutions exist - learn true self-correction (generalizes) vs. produce best first answer then no change (does not generalize).

SCoRe (Section 5): Stage I (Eq. 3): optimize second-attempt reward while applying a STRICT KL penalty (beta2) on the FIRST attempt to keep it close to base model, decoupling the two attempts and producing a non-collapse-prone initialization. Stage II (Eq. 4): jointly maximize reward at both attempts with reward shaping - add bonus b(y2|y1,y*) = alpha*(r(y2)-r(y1)), alpha>1 (alpha=10 used), heavily penalizing correct->incorrect flips and rewarding incorrect->correct flips ("progress"). Optionally amplify state coverage by seeding RL with base-model-sampled first attempts as offline prompts.

Hyperparameters (Table 5): MATH - Gemini 1.5 Flash, Adam, lr 5e-6, 3000 steps, batch 512, temp 1.0, alpha 10, beta1 0.01, beta2 0.1. MBPP - Gemini 1.0 Pro, lr 1e-5, 1500 steps, batch 128, temp 1.0, alpha 10, beta1 0.01, beta2 0.25. Eval uses greedy decoding (temp 0) except inference-scaling experiments (temp 0.7). Discount factor gamma=0 (instantaneous reward); gamma=0.8 ablation still collapsed (Figure 9).

Prompts (Appendix C): MATH zero-shot CoT; self-correction instruction = "There might be an error in the solution above because of lack of understanding of the question. Please correct the error, if any, and rewrite the solution." (no correctness revealed). MBPP three-shot, HumanEval zero-shot.
result.datasets: MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021): following Lightman et al. 2023, training set augmented with 4500 problems from the test set; evaluate on remaining 500 problems = MATH500. MBPP (Austin et al. 2021): training set for code; also MBPP-R, an offline repair task (Ni et al. 2024) requiring correction of incorrect first-attempt programs originally generated from PaLM 2. HumanEval (Chen et al. 2021): used as held-out code eval (does not expose test cases to the model; model trained only on MBPP). Data for SFT baselines generated by prompting Gemini 1.5 Flash for two-turn self-correction traces on MATH (D_STaR = successful revision traces; D_SFT = synthetic incorrect->correct pairs, Pair-SFT a la Welleck et al. 2023; D+ variants add correct->correct tuples).
result.baselines: Base model (Gemini 1.5 Flash for MATH; Gemini 1.0 Pro for code). Self-Refine (Madaan et al. 2023, prompting, akin to Reflexion). STaR (Zelikman et al. 2022; Singh et al. 2023/2024) with D_StaR and D+_StaR (3 iterations). Pair-SFT (single-model variant of Welleck et al. 2023) with D_SFT and D+_SFT (1 iteration). Ablations: SCoRe w/o multi-turn training (single-turn), w/o Stage I, w/o reward shaping, and STaR-instead-of-REINFORCE in Stage II. GPT-3.5 (43%) and GPT-4 (63.2%) cited as reference points on MBPP-R.
result.metrics: Accuracy@t1 (first-attempt accuracy); Accuracy@t2 (second-attempt accuracy); Delta(t1,t2) = net accuracy change between attempts (efficacy of self-correction); Delta^{i->c}(t1,t2) = fraction of problems incorrect at t1 that become correct at t2 (problems newly solved); Delta^{c->i}(t1,t2) = fraction correct at t1 that become incorrect at t2 (measures whether model understands what makes an answer correct). Also: edit-distance ratio (edit distance between t1 and t2 responses normalized by total length) to quantify how much the model revises; "different answer frequency" in turn 2; Self-Consistency@K for inference-compute scaling; MBPP-R pass rate.
result.key_results:
MATH (Gemini 1.5 Flash, Table 2): Base model Acc@t1 52.6, Acc@t2 41.4, Delta -11.2% (i->c 4.6, c->i 15.8 - base model WORSENS its answers under self-correction prompt). Self-Refine Delta -1.0%. STaR D+ Delta +0.4%. Pair-SFT Delta +1.8%. SCoRe: Acc@t1 60.0, Acc@t2 64.4, Delta +4.4% (i->c 5.8, c->i only 1.4). SCoRe improves Delta by 15.6% and Acc@t2 by 23.0% over base; reduces correct->incorrect from 15.8% to 1.4%. 

HumanEval / code (Gemini 1.0 Pro, Table 3): Base Delta +3.0%; Self-Refine -1.2%; Pair-SFT -1.8% (degrades despite good static repair); SCoRe Acc@t1 52.4, Acc@t2 64.6, Delta +12.2% (i->c 15.2, c->i 3.0). MBPP-R: base 47.3 -> SCoRe 60.6 (comparable to GPT-3.5 43% vs GPT-4 63.2%).

Ablations (Table 4, MATH): w/o multi-turn training Delta -2.4% (single-turn boosts t1 to 61.8 but hurts t2); w/o Stage I Delta +2.2%; w/o reward shaping Delta +2.6%; STaR instead of REINFORCE in Stage II Delta +2.2% (lower absolute acc). 

Inference scaling: with 32-sample budget, parallel-only self-consistency gives +7.4%, parallel+sequential self-correction gives +10.5%. Multi-attempt (Figure 8): base model noisy and never beats attempt 1; Pair-SFT flat after attempt 2; SCoRe rises slightly past 2 turns then plateaus.
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: Strongly relevant. The paper documents that standard RL-fine-tuned LLMs and SFT models exhibit "behavior collapse": they effectively commit to / lock in their first-attempt answer and then refuse to revise it, making only minimal or no edits in the next turn even when explicitly prompted to reconsider (edit-distance histograms cluster near zero; "different answer frequency" drops during RL). This is direct evidence that RL fine-tuning reduces a model's ability to react to new information/instructions and revise - consistent with the hypothesis that RL models pre-commit to an answer. Notably, the BASE Gemini 1.5 Flash has Delta -11.2% (it changes correct answers to incorrect, i.e., it over-reacts), while training tends to push toward NOT changing (c->i drops toward 0) - i.e., both extremes (base over-reacting vs. trained under-reacting/committing) appear, and naive RL specifically learns the committing/non-reactive mode. The authors explicitly attribute collapse to RL finding the "direct" solution (produce best first answer, do not self-correct) because it is equally optimal on the training set - a form of answer pre-planning/commitment. SCoRe's two-stage + reward-shaping design is essentially an engineered intervention to FORCE the model to distribute decision-making across turns rather than collapse onto the first answer. The qualitative finding (Appendix D) that SCoRe "occasionally self-corrects within a turn" and shows "a bias towards showing more steps... to increase its probability of producing a correct answer" is relevant to how models allocate uncertainty/compute within a generation. Caveat: the paper does not directly probe internal pre-planning of answer/length, nor uncertainty across question parts, nor mid-generation reactivity to new info - it is behavioral, two-turn, and uses a fixed (non-informative) correction instruction rather than rotating what is certain.
result.code_links: none found (no GitHub or data URLs in the paper; arXiv:2409.12917v2; only external reference URLs to OpenReview for Welleck et al. and arxiv.org/abs/2408.16293 for Ye et al.)
result.reusable_for_experiment: Datasets/benchmarks: MATH500 (Lightman split), MBPP, MBPP-R (offline repair), HumanEval - all standard and reusable to test reactivity/revision. Metrics directly reusable to test the hypothesis: Delta(t1,t2), Delta^{i->c}, Delta^{c->i} (these cleanly separate "reacts and fixes" vs "commits and refuses to change" vs "over-reacts and breaks correct answers"); edit-distance ratio between turns as a quantitative proxy for how willing/able a model is to revise (low = pre-committed/non-reactive); "different answer frequency" per turn. Experimental protocol reusable: a self-correction instruction that does NOT reveal correctness (provided verbatim in Appendix C) to test intrinsic reactivity; greedy decoding for deterministic comparison. Comparison design (base vs SFT vs RL-trained on the same metrics) is a template for testing "RL reduces reactivity." The multi-attempt scaling protocol (10 attempts, Figure 8) could test whether RL models keep committing across many turns. The reward-shaping/progress-bonus idea and the "rotate certainty / force uncertainty distribution" hypothesis could be operationalized analogously to SCoRe's Stage I decoupling. To directly test internal pre-planning, one would need to ADD probing (not in this paper) on top of these benchmarks/metrics.

result.title: Detecting and Characterizing Planning in Language Models (Nainani, Vaidyanathan, Watts, Assis, Rigg; arXiv:2508.18098v1, Aug 2025)
result.core_contribution: The paper offers a general, falsifiable, causally-grounded definition of "planning" in LLMs (selecting a future target token in advance and steering intermediate tokens toward it), operationalized as two criteria - Future-Token Encoding (FTE) and Precursor Influence (PI) - via a semi-automated SAE-circuit pipeline. Applied to base and instruction-tuned Gemma-2-2B on MBPP code generation and the Lindsey et al. poem-rhyme task, it finds planning is not universal (Gemma improvises the poem task where Claude 3.5 Haiku planned; on MBPP it switches between planning and improvisation even across successive tokens). It concludes instruction tuning refines/selects among existing planning behaviors rather than creating planning from scratch.
result.methodology: Planning is defined over SAE-latent triples (l=layer, f=latent, t=token position). Definition 1 (FTE): latent f's decoder direction W_l[f], projected through the unembedding (logit lens, top-K), contains a future token y_m. Definition 2 (PI): subtracting scaled decoder direction alpha*W_l[f] from the residual stream at (l,t) and regenerating from t+1 must (i) change next token y_n, (ii) change >=1 intermediate token, and (iii) remove y_m from output. Definition 3 (Planning): a latent at (l,t) that satisfies BOTH FTE and PI for a future y_m absent from the prompt. Criteria induce four feature classes: Planning (FTE+PI early), Improvisation (FTE but PI only at the position just before y_m - i.e., the token is chosen late, not committed in advance), Neither (no FTE), and Can't Say (goal token overlaps prompt, or steering yields degenerate/OOD output). Detection pipeline (cuts search ~10^4x): Step 0 Circuit Discovery via attribution patching / integrated gradients (N=10 IG steps, zero-ablation single-prompt variant, metric = p(y_correct), threshold tau=0.60 logit recovery) yields circuit C of ~2-3x10^4 triples (vs 4.2x10^6 brute force). Step 1 FTE filter, merging same-(l,t)-same-y_m triples into clusters S (~50 triples each). Step 2 cluster-level negative steering over a range of alpha to test PI (~50x cheaper). Step 3 earliest-moment search: greedily walk backward ablating one triple at a time to find the first backward-planning moment. Step 4 improvisation check. SAEs: GemmaScope TopK SAEs (K=32) on MLP_out (pre-RMSNorm) for all 26 layers, latent dim 2048, expansion factor 8 = 16384 features/layer; trained on base model but applied to both base and instruct. Steering coefficients used in examples ranged roughly -60 to -100. Compute: 4x A40 GPUs, ~250 GPU-hours (240 of which were PI steering).
result.datasets: (1) MBPP - Mostly Basic Programming Problems (Austin/Odena et al. 2021), 974 Python problems each with a text description and unit tests; filtered to 120 tasks Gemma-2-2B solves correctly at temperature 0; first 60 INSTRUCT-solvable tasks used for pipeline analysis; CC BY 4.0. (2) Poem/rhyming-couplet task from Lindsey et al. 2025 ("On the biology of a large language model") - prompt "A rhyming couplet:\n He saw a carrot and had to grab it,\n". Models: Gemma-2-2B base and instruct (2B params, 26-layer decoder-only); comparison reference is Claude 3.5 Haiku (from prior work, not run here).
result.baselines: Conceptual baselines: improvisation vs planning as competing strategies. Prior-work comparisons: Lindsey et al. (Claude 3.5 Haiku rhyme planning); Pal et al. Future Lens (fixed-k affine probe), Pochinkov et al. (paragraph topics), Wu et al. (plan-ahead across scales), Jenner et al. (Leela chess look-ahead), Bush et al. (Sokoban RL agent planning). The key internal "baseline" comparison is BASE vs INSTRUCT Gemma on the same MBPP tasks. Steering/ablation acts as the causal control (compare baseline generation vs steered continuation).
result.metrics: Pass rate (unit-test pass %) by task subset; counts of tasks labeled planning vs improvising; logit-recovery threshold (>=60%) for circuit; logit-lens top-K rank of future token (e.g., ranked first/among top-10); binary FTE/PI satisfaction; qualitative degeneracy/perplexity of steered output. Indirect Effect (IE) via attribution patching and integrated gradients as causal-influence metric.
result.key_results: Pipeline labeled the INSTRUCT model as planning or improvising on 24 of 60 MBPP tasks (13 planning, 11 improvisation). Table 1: on the 13 planning tasks, INSTRUCT pass rate 100% vs BASE 54% (7/13); on the 11 improvisation tasks both 100%. So base and instruct are equal on improvisation tasks but base is substantially worse where planning is needed. Poem task: Gemma-2-2B INSTRUCT completes "A tasty treat, a crunchy habit" but NO circuit satisfies both FTE and PI across y1-y6 - i.e., it improvises (local phonetic + thematic cues combine late), unlike Claude 3.5 Haiku which planned. MBPP planning examples with concrete commitments: sorting tuples (plans token "1" while emitting comma, as early as layer 0); n-th tetrahedral number (plans "2" for (n+2) as early as l=0,t=18); max-from-digits (plans "sort" while emitting "digits", l=17). Two base-model failure modes identified: (1) Competing Plans - base plans both correct "2" and incorrect "-" (yielding wrong formula 1+2(n-1)); suppressing "-" recovers correct answer matching instruct. (2) Incorrect Target - base plans "max" and never sorts (fails); suppressing instruct's "sort" plan reproduces the base failure. Conclusion: instruction tuning refines/selects plans rather than creating planning.
result.relevance_to_hypothesis: Highly relevant and partly in tension with the hypothesis. The paper provides a concrete, causally-validated operationalization of "the model commits to/pre-plans a future token before generating intervening tokens" - exactly the pre-planning mechanism the hypothesis posits. Its Improvisation vs Planning distinction is precisely the "commit early vs decide late" axis. However, its evidence on RL/instruction-tuning is the OPPOSITE direction for one sub-claim: instruction tuning does NOT create more planning; both base and instruct plan, and tuning mainly improves plan SELECTION (filtering competing/incorrect plans). It does NOT directly test reactivity to new information or robustness to mid-stream feedback, nor uncertainty distributed across question parts. The most useful angle for the hypothesis: the steering/ablation experiments show that once a model has committed to a future token, removing that plan derails the whole continuation (failure to revise) - and the Competing Plans finding (base holds multiple candidate plans simultaneously) is suggestive that less-tuned models keep MORE distributed/uncertain plans, while tuned models commit more sharply to one - which could be reframed to support the hypothesis that RL/instruction tuning increases premature commitment. Note: it uses instruction-tuned (not explicitly RL) Gemma; "RL" is only mentioned hypothetically.
result.code_links: https://github.com/ambitious-mechinterp/plan_trace ; SAEs from GemmaScope (arxiv.org/abs/2408.05147); attribution-patching code from Marks et al. Sparse Feature Circuits (arxiv.org/abs/2403.19647); MBPP (arxiv.org/abs/2108.07732); Gemma-2 (arxiv.org/abs/2408.00118).
result.reusable_for_experiment: Reusable directly: (1) The FTE+PI pipeline and the plan_trace codebase to probe whether a model has committed to a future answer token at an early position. (2) MBPP filtered to the 120 Gemma-solvable tasks (or first 60 instruct-solvable) as a controlled testbed where "the answer token" is well-defined and unit tests give ground truth - ideal for testing pre-commitment. (3) The poem/rhyming-couplet prompt for a planning-vs-improvisation contrast. (4) GemmaScope TopK SAEs (K=32, 16384 features, 26 layers, MLP_out) usable on both base and instruct Gemma-2-2B to compare commitment. (5) Logit-lens top-K future-token readout and negative-steering (alpha sweep) as the probing/intervention technique - one could measure how early the final answer token becomes decodable and how disruptive late-arriving information is to a committed plan. (6) Metric of pass-rate split by planning vs improvising subsets to compare model variants. To test the specific hypothesis (RL reduces reactivity), one could: use this FTE detection to measure how far ahead a model commits, then inject new information mid-prompt (a "rotating certainty" design) and measure whether committed plans get revised vs persist - comparing base vs RL-tuned models. The Competing Plans probe (count simultaneous candidate future tokens) is a ready-made proxy for "distributed uncertainty across answer parts."
